title: Circadian rhythms of hosts and their gut microbiomes: Implications for animal physiology and ecology
authors: Sato, M.; Klein, R.; Fernandez, L.
abstract: Daily rhythms in host physiology influence microbial communities, and microbial activity may in turn shape host metabolism, behavior, and ecological interactions.

sections.heading: Why it matters
sections.body: The paper connects chronobiology with ecological context, showing how time of day can change the interpretation of host-microbe data.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.body: The authors combine time-series sampling, metagenomic profiling, and controlled feeding schedules to separate host-driven and microbial effects.

sections.heading: Reading hint
sections.body: Students who care about methods can focus on the sampling design first, then return to the ecological interpretation later.

title: Bone density and the lightweight skeletons of birds
authors: Nguyen, T.; Patel, A.; Hoshino, Y.
abstract: Bird skeletons are often described as lightweight, but density, internal structure, and mechanical strength vary across species and flight styles.

sections.heading: Core question
sections.body: The paper asks whether lightness is best understood as low density, altered geometry, or optimized internal architecture.

sections.heading: Methods
sections.body: Micro-CT scans and comparative measurements are used to compare limb bones across flying and non-flying taxa.

sections.heading: Reading hint
sections.body: The figures carry much of the argument. Read the figure captions before reading the full discussion.

title: Reduced seed dispersal potential in dandelions isolated within urban historical sites
authors: Arai, K.; Wilson, J.; Morita, N.
abstract: Urban isolation can alter traits related to dispersal, potentially reducing the ability of plant populations to move across fragmented landscapes.

sections.heading: Study system
sections.body: The authors compare dandelion populations across connected urban green spaces and isolated historical sites.

sections.heading: Key result
sections.body: Seeds from isolated populations show reduced traits associated with wind dispersal, suggesting local selection or demographic effects.

sections.heading: Reading hint
sections.body: Focus on how the paper links trait measurements to ecological interpretation, not only on the statistical significance.

title: Student-generated questions improve retention in introductory physics laboratories
abstract: Structured opportunities for students to write their own questions during laboratory work can improve conceptual retention and ownership of inquiry.

sections.heading: Design
sections.body: The intervention places question writing before formal explanation, making students' uncertainty part of the learning process.

sections.heading: Evidence
sections.body: Retention gains are strongest for students who revise questions after peer discussion.

sections.heading: Reading hint
sections.body: This paper is useful when thinking about PaperTrail's educational philosophy and how prompts should invite autonomy.
